title: "We can break even and be self-sustaining within Riot" – The firm behind League of Legends is "super close" to making esports sustainable
summary: "We can break even and be self-sustaining within Riot" – The firm behind League of Legends is "super close" to making esports sustainable &nbs...
content:
Riot Games' publishing and esports boss, John Needham, on the journey to profitability, Riot's transmedia ambitions, and why it allowed gambling sponsorship

Riot Games' esports business still isn't profitable.

This has been the case for a long time, and historically it hasn'tnecessarilybeen a problem for the League of Legends giant; at various points in the past, then-head of esports John Needham explained that his firmwanted to ensure the broader ecosystem, including the various esports teams, was profitable. But the aim was for esports to be a sustainable business in its own right.

That business is now "super close" to being profitable. What's changed? Needham –now head of publishing and esports at Riot– says the firm has adjusted how it operates its pro-gaming business since 2020.

"Coming out of the pandemic, we were in this esports winter," he tellsGamesIndustry.biz.

"The sponsorship business was weak, teams were having a hard time getting capital and investment in their business. We pivoted our business model away from sponsorships to digital items; that was a big positive move for teams. Teams also adjusted how much they were spending. The team business is in as good a place as it has ever been, now, and we are trying to do other things to allow teams to make alternate sources of revenue."

title: LoL Esports
content:
The competitive League of Legends ecosystem is buzzing following the latest announcement regarding 'LoL Esports'. As the season progresses, this development has quickly taken center stage, drawing intense scrutiny from fans, analysts, and professional players alike. The core update highlights a significant shift that is expected to have wide-ranging consequences for the regional leagues and international tournaments.

Riot Games continues to refine the competitive structure, balancing the game's high skill ceiling with spectator entertainment. The professional scene is an ever-evolving battleground where even the smallest adjustment can make or break a team's championship aspirations. Every coach, analyst, and player must stay ahead of the curve to remain competitive.

Speaking on the recent events, seasoned esports analysts noted that 'LoL Esports  LoL Esports' is not just a temporary trend but a fundamental shift in how teams will approach their upcoming matchdays. 'Draft priority and map control are going to be completely redefined over the next few weeks,' remarked a prominent league caster. 'Teams that fail to recognize the gravity of this change will find themselves falling behind very quickly in the standings.'

As the community awaits the upcoming weekend matches, anticipation continues to build. Fans can catch all the high-stakes action live on official streaming channels, complete with expert desk analysis and live regional broadcasts. With so much on the line, the next chapter of the 2026 competitive season promises to deliver some of the most memorable moments in League of Legends esports history.

title: Bilibili Gaming surge ahead at Mid-Season Invitational
summary: Bilibili Gaming surge ahead at Mid-Season Invitational  Field Level Media
content:
Bilibili Gaming swept LYON on Monday to reach the upper-bracket final of the League of Legends Mid-Season Invitational in Daejeon, South Korea.

Bilibili Gaming recorded 32-, 29- and 29-minute wins on blue, led by China’s Zhuo “Knight” Ding with a 20-7-25 kills-deaths-assists ratio.

Bilibili Gaming will face Hanwha Life, with the winner advancing to Sunday’s grand final of the $2 million tournament. The champion will take home $500,000 and also qualify for this fall’s 2026 World Championship.

LYON dropped to the lower bracket, where T1 survived on Monday with a sweep against FURIA.

T1 posted 34-, 29- and 35-minute victories on blue to knock out FURIA. South Korea’s Soo-hwan “Peyz” Kim paced T1 with a 26-5-18 kills-deaths-assists ratio.

The upcoming Round 2 matches in the lower bracket will pit T1 against G2 Esports and Lyon against Secret Whales.

title: 18th President's Cup Amateur Esports Tournament Kicks Off with Regional Qualifiers
summary: 18th President's Cup Amateur Esports Tournament Kicks Off with Regional Qualifiers  Inven Global
content:
The Korea e-Sports Association (KeSPA, President Kim Young-man) announced on Wednesday, the 8th, that the regional qualifiers for the '18th President's Cup Amateur Esports Tournament' (President's Cup KeG) will begin across the country on Friday, July 17.

Hosted by the Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism and organized by the Korea e-Sports Association, the President's Cup KeG is Korea's first national-level official amateur esports tournament, launched in 2007 to promote balanced regional development and expand the amateur esports base. Since being elevated to a President's Cup event in 2009, it has remained the only government-backed national amateur esports tournament to be held consistently for 20 years.

To date, approximately 37k amateur players have participated in the President's Cup KeG, with over 180 advancing to the professional stage. Notably, last year saw 9 League of Legends players and 2 FC Online players debut as professionals, proving that the President's Cup KeG has established itself as a premier gateway for amateur talent.

The 18th President's Cup KeG will feature the same four official titles as last year: ▲Eternal Return (Nimble Neuron), ▲Brawl Stars (Supercell), ▲FC Online (Nexon Korea), and ▲League of Legends (Riot Games). ▲Puyo Puyo eSports (Sega) has been newly adopted as a strategic title. As Puyo Puyo eSports is an official event for the 2026 Aichi-Nagoya Asian Games, this tournament is expected to further revitalize the domestic esports scene.

Regional qualifiers to select representatives for the national finals will be held sequentially in local governments across the country, starting with Jeju Special Self-Governing Province on Friday, July 17, and concluding on Sunday, August 2. Qualifiers will be held online or offline depending on local circumstances, with each region selecting 1 team (3-4 members) for Eternal Return, 1 team (3 members) for Brawl Stars, 2 players for FC Online, and 1 team (5 members) for League of Legends. Further details are available on the official website (keg.e-sports.or.kr). Additionally, for the strategic title Puyo Puyo eSports, a separate online preliminary round will be held on Sunday, July 19, to select 8 finalists for the national championship.

The President's Cup KeG national finals, where regional representatives gather, will be held on Saturday, August 15, and Sunday, August 16, at the Gyeongju Indoor Gymnasium in Gyeongju, Gyeongsangbuk-do. The region that wins the overall championship will be awarded the championship trophy and the honor of receiving the President's Award. The Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism Award will be presented to the winning region for each official title. Furthermore, the top two finishers in FC Online will be granted a seed for the quarterfinals of the 'FC ONLINE FUTURES LEAGUE (FFL).' The total prize pool is ₩25.5 million.

title: H1 2026: Top Esports Games & Tournaments by Viewership
summary: H1 2026: Top Esports Games & Tournaments by Viewership  Esports Charts
content:
According to Esports Charts data, Counter-Strike overtook League of Legends as the most popular esports title of the first half of 2026. The record-breaking IEM Cologne Major 2026, which reached 2.75 million Peak Viewers, was the key reason behind this achievement. However, League of Legends fans have little reason to worry — the game's biggest tournaments of the year, including the 2026 World Championship, are still ahead.

Mobile Legends: Bang Bangretained its position as the most popular esports title of the first half of 2026, largely thanks to theM7 World Championship, the game's premier tournament, whichtook place in January.

Counter-Strikeclimbed to second place in the ranking. Valve’s iconic shooter reached 2.75 million Peak Viewers during the Grand Final betweenFURIAandTeam Falcons. As expected, the championship match also setnew all-time viewership recordsfor both finalist organizations. Don't forget to explore the tournament's detailed viewership statistics in theEvent Dashboard.

League of Legendsslipped to third place in the rankings during the second quarter. However, the game's peak viewership actually increased,reaching 2.27 million Peak Viewersduring the iconic Korean showdown betweenT1andGen.Gon the final day of theLCK 2026 Road to MSIqualifier. After a hard-fought 3:2 victory, T1 secured the last available spot atMSI 2026, while Gen.G(the reigningMSI 2025champions) failed to qualify and will not have the opportunity to defend their title on home soil.

Two more titles surpassed the one million Peak Viewers milestone by the middle of the year:PUBG MobileandVALORANT. The biggest events for these games so far have been thePUBG Mobile Global Open 2026 Season 1andVALORANT Masters London 2026, which delivered nearly identical peak viewership figures. However, even higher numbers are likely still to come, as both games will hold their flagship world championship events during the second half of the year.

The M7 World Championship remains the most-popular esports tournament of 2026 so far, but the second place went toMPL Indonesia Season 17. The Grand Final betweenONIC EsportsandBigetron by Vitalityattracted more than 2.94 million Peak Viewers. While impressive, the result fell well short of the league's all-time record and marked a second consecutive season of declining peak viewership following the exceptionally successfulSeason 15. There was still one milestone to celebrate, however: the Season 17 Grand Final not only secured Bigetron by Vitality's first major championship, but also became the most-watched match in the organization's history.
